title: Goose Fat Roast Potatoes
subtitle: Parboiled, roughed up, and roasted in a very hot pan of smoking goose fat
excerpt: The crispiest roast potatoes come from two things: parboiling to fluff the surface, and roasting in a fat that is already smoking hot when the potatoes go in. Goose fat, with its high smoke point and rich flavour, produces a golden crust unlike any other fat.
sourceNotes: Delia Smith, Complete Cookery Course (1978): parboil and fluff method for roast potatoes. Nigella Lawson, How to Eat (1998): goose fat and par-cooking notes. Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861, public domain): an early description of the parboil-then-roast method for potatoes. Traditional British Sunday lunch practice.

content.text: Perfectly crisp roast potatoes require two steps that cannot be skipped. First,
parboiling
in salted water until the edges are soft enough to crumble. Second,
chuffing
: shaking the drained potatoes in the saucepan with the lid on until the surface is visibly roughed up and floury. Those rough edges absorb the hot fat and become the crust.

Goose fat (sold in jars in most supermarkets) is the classic choice. Duck fat is interchangeable. Lard produces equally good results at lower cost. Whatever fat you use, it must be in a hot roasting pan and smoking before the potatoes go in.
What you need

items.name: Potato
items.prepNote: Maris Piper or King Edward (approx. 1.5 kg), peeled and cut into even chunks

items.name: Duck fat
items.prepNote: goose fat or duck fat, from a jar

items.name: Sea salt flakes
items.prepNote: 1 tsp for parboiling water, 1 tsp to finish

content.text: Method
Parboil and chuff

Put the potato chunks into a large saucepan, cover with cold salted water, and bring to the boil. Simmer for 8-10 minutes until the edges are soft and beginning to crumble when pressed. Drain completely in a colander, return to the dry saucepan, and shake vigorously with the lid on for 30 seconds. The surfaces should look rough and floury.
Heat the fat and roast
Heat the oven to 220°C (200°C fan). Put the
{{duck-fat}} of goose fat

in a large roasting pan and place in the oven for 10-15 minutes until the fat is smoking. Working quickly and carefully, tip the potatoes into the hot fat; they will spit. Turn each piece so all surfaces are coated. Add the garlic cloves and rosemary if using. Roast for 45-55 minutes, turning once after 30 minutes, until deep golden and crisp.
Season and serve
Drain the potatoes briefly on kitchen paper and season with a final pinch of
{{sea-salt-flakes}}
. Serve immediately; roast potatoes do not hold well.

rows.fix: The fat must be visibly smoking. Chuff the potatoes until the surface looks floury and rough, not just damp.
rows.cause: Fat was not hot enough when the potatoes went in, or the surface was not roughed up sufficiently
rows.symptom: Potatoes are soft rather than crisp

rows.fix: Parboil for 8 minutes, not 12. The edges should soften and crumble, but the centre should still be firm.
rows.cause: Parboiled for too long, or variety was too floury

rows.fix: Cut chunks to a consistent size of 5-6 cm. Reduce oven to 200°C and add 10 minutes.
rows.cause: Oven too hot, or chunks were too large
rows.symptom: Potatoes are burnt on the outside but raw inside

rows.fix: Use a large pan and leave space between each piece. Two separate pans is better than overcrowding one.
rows.cause: Too many potatoes in the pan, causing steaming rather than roasting

content.text: Where this dish lives

Goose fat roast potatoes are the defining side dish of the British Sunday roast. The fat is available year-round in jars, but goose-fat leftovers from a roast goose or Christmas dinner are prized: potted and refrigerated, they keep for weeks. The parboil and chuff technique produces the same result regardless of which fat you use, and the method has not changed in principle since the mid-19th century.
